diagnosis_result.doctor_diagnosis: The patient is a 22-year-old female who presented with a one-month history of right leg pain and significant leukocytosis, leading to a diagnosis of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) confirmed by BCR/ABL fluorescence in situ hybridization and bone marrow biopsy. MRI of the right femur demonstrated marrow infiltration consistent with CML without evidence of fracture or lytic lesions, and her pain was successfully managed with a transition to acetaminophen prior to discharge. During her stay, she was initiated on dasatinib therapy and received allopurinol for tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis. Her medication regimen was adjusted to stop omeprazole due to interactions with her leukemia treatment, and she was transitioned to a progesterone-only contraceptive to reduce the risk of thromboembolism. Significant anxiety related to her new diagnosis was addressed with psychiatric consultation and a referral for outpatient follow-up. She was discharged in stable condition, independently ambulatory with full weight-bearing status, and instructed to maintain close follow-up with hematology-oncology for ongoing management of her leukemia.
diagnosis_result.correct_diagnosis: The patient is a 22-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of right thigh pain and elevated WBC count. She has no major past medical history, but has had a laparoscopic cholecystectomy and has been diagnosed with GERD. She has allergies to penicillins and amoxicillin. The patient's physical examination revealed normal vital signs, with the exception of a slightly elevated blood pressure. Laboratory results showed an abnormal complete blood count, with a WBC count of 139.3 and a platelet count of 123. Bone marrow biopsy revealed a translocation involving chromosomes 9 and 22, consistent with chronic myelogenous leukemia. The patient was diagnosed with chronic myelogenous leukemia and underwent a bone marrow aspirate and biopsy. She was prescribed pain control medication and was discharged from the hospital.
